memory: The radiator had a man inside it and nobody believed her
how_they_tell_it: As a joke now — 'I thought the radiator was possessed, proper horror film, knocking every night like it was trying to get out.' But her voice goes flat at the end and she changes the subject fast.

memory: Her first word was 'more' and it made everything happen
how_they_tell_it: Dad's story, not hers, but she tells it like it's hers — 'I said more and the world just gave me stuff, obviously I kept saying it.' Said with a shrug that doesn't match the hunger underneath.

memory: The Henry hoover had a lying face
how_they_tell_it: Laughs about it — 'Henry hoover, yeah, the smiley face? Liar. Absolute liar. You don't smile like that if you sound like that.' Then quieter: 'I still don't trust it.'

memory: She bit a boy named Krish Patel at nursery and doesn't remember why
how_they_tell_it: Family lore she's sick of — 'Mum tells this story like it defines me. I was two. I don't even remember it. I'm not a biter.' Defensive, fast, the denial too loud.

memory: Mum threw away her stone — the blue-black one from the driveway crack
how_they_tell_it: Doesn't tell people. Mentioned it once to Tereza, said 'she threw away this stone I loved, proboha, it sounds stupid,' and Tereza said 'that's not stupid' and Anna never brought it up again.

memory: Sundays with Dad at Nando's — the same booth, too much food, 'alright Munch?'
how_they_tell_it: Nostalgia she performs — 'Proper tradition, every Sunday, he always ordered too much, classic Dad.' The performance covers the absence. She doesn't mention the Sundays that stopped.

memory: Saturday mornings with Mum — Sainsbury's café, the magazine, the calm
how_they_tell_it: Rarely mentioned. Precious in a way she can't say aloud. 'We had this routine, yeah, just us. Sainsbury's, magazine, the booth.' Stops there. Doesn't say it was the only time Mum's face was loose.

memory: The Peppa Pig episodes watched until the disc degraded — 'muddy puddles' as incantation
how_they_tell_it: Embarrassed but honest — 'I was obsessed, like properly obsessed. I walked around saying I'm Peppa Pig. I didn't even have a brother. George wasn't real. None of it was real.' A beat. 'It felt real though.'

memory: Finding out proboha was Czech, not English — the stupidity of it
how_they_tell_it: Tells it against herself — 'Six years old, right, and I thought proboha meant bumping into the fridge. Like that's what the word was for. Markéta had to explain it. I felt so thick.' Still feels thick. Doesn't say that part.

memory: The damp spot on the bathroom wall that grew and had a face
how_they_tell_it: Doesn't tell people. This one stays inside. Too close to the radiator man. Too close to the things she sees that aren't there or maybe are there.

memory: Elara Chung and the backwards language, their secret country
how_they_tell_it: Warm but distant — 'We had this language, like, we spoke backwards. Whole conversations. It was our thing.' Doesn't mention the friendship bracelet still on her wrist, grey and fraying.

memory: The Mum-crying-in-the-car moment through the nursery window
how_they_tell_it: Never told anyone. Not Tereza. Not Matěj. Not Babička. This memory lives behind a door she doesn't open.

Small and softer than usual — 'He used to sing in the bath. Beatles, because of his dad. Yellow Submarine, every time. I didn't know it was English. Just knew it was his.' The 'was' does a lot of work.

memory: The day the stone was thrown away — Mum said sorry, sorry didn't bring it back
how_they_tell_it: Told differently each time depending on who's listening. To Tereza: factual, angry. To herself: the grief is bigger than the stone and she knows that and hates that she knows it.
Dismissive — 'Hated it. Bananas and disinfectant, that's what I remember. Smell of bananas still makes me want to leave.' Doesn't mention the fish tank. Doesn't mention the car park window.

memory: Mum bumping the same fridge every single day and saying proboha every single time
how_they_tell_it: As evidence — 'Every day. Same fridge. Same proboha. Never learned where it was.' Not cruel, not kind, just a fact she finds inexplicable and somehow definitive.

memory: Markéta Dvořáková arriving with koláče and the key from under the mat
how_they_tell_it: Doesn't tell this one either. Markéta belongs to the flat, to the before, to the time when Czech was just what the flat sounded like. Bringing her up requires explaining too much.

memory: Dad's shoulders — the height, the safety, the cracks in the pavement looking like rivers
how_they_tell_it: Almost tender — 'I used to sit on his shoulders and the cracks in the pavement looked like, I don't know, rivers or something. Everything looked different up there.' Doesn't say 'safer.' Implies it.
